Includes motor purge/enclosure pressurisation control system (where fitted)";"The strategy for electrical motor maintenance comprises regular online visual inspections and basic condition checks carried by operations staff during normal plant surveillance and:
-	Vibration analysis – where justified and feasible
-	Electrical Testing such as balancing motor fields
-	Thermography. (Only possible where IR window is present).
-	Oil analysis – depending on type of lubrication and size of oil inventory and where justified
-	Partial discharge. (HV motor which cabinet is fitted with designated ports)
-	Periodic offline detailed inspections.
-	Periodic offline electrical testing such as balancing motor fields
Note:
1. Motors typically carry Ex designations and thus are also subject to periodic inspection under the remit of the Ex strategy to verify their ongoing integrity.

2. Regular changeover of duty/standby units can be used to mitigate the effects of fretting and false brinelling that can occur on stationary bearings that are subject to ambient vibration. The need for formally scheduling such changeover
column3: depends upon the prevailing equipment operating philosophy. It has been assumed that duty/standby equipment will be periodically changed over as a matter of course to balance individual item operating run hours.";"AC motors
column4: HV and LV and DC Motors drive equipment such as Pumps
column5: Fans and compressors etc. which are connected to the motor by an axle and coupling
column6: Pulley and V-belt or gearbox with the desired speed and power by converting Electrical power into rotational mechanical power.

column1: The current runs in stator windings and creates in the rotor an electro-magnetic force.
This force creates a torque thus the rotation of the rotor.
Large motor can be fitted with coolers to control the enclosure temperature. Also an anti-condensation heater can be fitted to prevent moisture or water droplets on the windings.
Large motor are using journal bearing while smaller one are using roller bearing either permanently lubricated or requiring greasing.";"DR";"EEDB

column1: Task to be scheduled (where applicable) to coincide with inspection/electrical testing of associated field control station and motor/field control station EX integrity checks.

column2: task should also be carried out in conjunction with relevant starter/MCC/LCP and feeder breaker.";"Electric motors are normally very reliable and failures that do occur are usually attributable to reasons other than electrical (e.g. most serious machine failures are where rotors and stators sustain damage are a direct result of a mechanical failure such as a bearing collapse). For this reason
column3: motors with a higher criticality will normally be selected for inclusion within the vibration monitoring program.

column1: The initial selected inspection/electrical testing interval of 3 years for high criticality LV motors is principally directed at detecting early symptoms of insulation degradation.
The five-yearly frequency for medium criticality motors is derived from the assumption of a nominal 20-year service life
column2: and hence a five-year maintenance period provides a comparable safe life of 25%.";"";"";"60";"Month";"36";"Month"

column1: ;This applies to those motors that employ pressurisation as a method of equipment protection within hazardous areas (i.e. Ex ‘p’ protection scheme). column2: such pressurisation systems incorporate certain control elements and specifics that warrant supplementary maintenance attention.

column1: Purge/pressurisation control system vendors generally recommend formal checks of their equipment every six months. However
column2: the initial selected interval of 12 months reflects typical practice within the oil and gas industry.
